quotes: I used to work for Florida Technical College as the Program Director for Criminal Justice, IT and Cybersecurity
They didn't have a Cybersecurity program when I started and so I was responsible for helping to build a program from the ground up
I had to find the subject matter experts, I had to deal with the budget, I had to coordinate all of the design of the courses being created
That included going through any of the certifications that we're going to be able to be taken after they go through the program. So Cybersecurity Plus, CompTIA, A-plus
role_fit_and_relevant_experience.rationale: Cynthia held a Program Director role — clearly above classroom teaching — and personally built a new Cybersecurity program from scratch, managing budget, SME coordination, course design, and certification alignment. She also oversaw Allied Health and other pathways beyond her core expertise, and describes a crosswalk process at University of Phoenix aligning curriculum to hospital/industry requirements. This is direct, program-level CTE experience with compliance and certification navigation, meeting the bar for a 4.

quotes: going through any of the certifications that we're going to be able to be taken after they go through the program. So Cybersecurity Plus, CompTIA, A-plus

I worked very closely with the hospitals to see what they wanted for individuals that would be graduating from our program and then we did a crosswalk of aligning our curriculum with their requirements
it's really about making sure that you do a gap analysis to align your course curriculum with any certification or any industry standards
domain_judgment.rationale: Cynthia demonstrates solid understanding that CTE courses must satisfy certification body criteria and industry/articulation partner requirements, and she names specific certification bodies (CompTIA, A+, PHR). She describes a crosswalk methodology against external requirements. However, she does not name or distinguish Perkins funding requirements, state CTE standards frameworks, or articulation agreement mechanics specifically — the layered compliance landscape (funding vs. certification vs. state standards) is not explicitly differentiated. This is a confident 3: specific and grounded, but not the layered multi-framework fluency of a 4.

quotes: I had to start with what the certification requirements were, align what the course teachings are with the certification
make sure that you do a gap analysis to align your course curriculum with any certification or any industry standards to make sure that you hit all those targets
I leaned very heavily on subject matter experts... I worked very closely with those individuals
process_and_methodology.rationale: Cynthia describes a clear multi-step review approach: identify certification/industry requirements, perform a gap analysis, align curriculum to those requirements, and leverage SMEs for areas outside her expertise. For Q3 specifically, she articulates a gap analysis framework and the concept of hitting all compliance targets. The process is repeatable and covers multiple requirement types. What keeps this from a 4 is the absence of explicit discussion of how gaps become prioritized, actionable revision instructions for a writing team — she describes the analysis but not the downstream communication of findings.

quotes: So again, with this one is all about alignment
it's really about making sure that you do a gap analysis to align your course curriculum with any certification or any industry standards
I leaned very heavily on subject matter experts
communication.rationale: Cynthia's answers are followable and organized — she addresses each question in sequence, provides concrete examples, and lands on clear takeaways. The delivery is conversational and occasionally circles back, but the through-line is trackable without re-listening. Spoken filler is present but not disqualifying. She does not demonstrate the crisp, precise language that would earn a 4 — some answers meander before landing — but the substance is clear and the listener can follow each answer's arc.

quotes: I'm going to start with the first question
The next question asked me to answer a time where I had to deal with a program that I may not have known as well
In regards to the final question on drafting a CTE course and what would I check to ensure that it is done right?
instruction_following_and_professionalis.rationale: Cynthia explicitly addresses all three questions in order, signals each transition, and provides a concrete example for each. Tone is professional and prepared. The answers are substantive, not thin. She falls just short of a 4 because the depth on Q3 (turning gaps into actionable revision direction for a writing team) is somewhat surface-level compared to the specificity she brings to Q1.

recommendation_reason: Total score 16 meets the threshold (>=14) and Role Fit 4 meets the gating criterion (>=3).
overall_summary: Cynthia Taylor is a strong candidate with genuine program-director-level CTE experience, including building a Cybersecurity program from scratch, managing certification alignment (CompTIA, A+, PHR), and overseeing multiple pathways beyond her core expertise. Her domain knowledge is solid and specific, though she does not explicitly engage with Perkins funding or state CTE standards frameworks by name. Her process methodology is clear and repeatable — gap analysis against certification/industry requirements — but she does not fully articulate how she would translate gaps into prioritized, actionable revision notes for a writing team. Communication is conversational but followable. Overall a strong candidate who likely scores in the advance range. Minor uncertainty stems from the audio-to-text transcript quality and the absence of explicit Perkins/state standards discussion, which slightly limits confidence in the domain judgment score.

limitations: Transcription quality may affect scoring accuracy.
Scores reflect rubric criteria only, not holistic assessment.
